topic: Two friends plan separate birthday parties on the same day
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crumpled napkin lay on the kitchen counter like a discarded battlefield. Mark stared at the calendar, his jaw tight enough to crack a tooth. "Are you serious?" he finally managed, his voice dangerously low. Across the room, Sarah was cheerfully arranging streamers, a bright, oblivious smile plastered on her face. He felt a hot, prickly sensation rising up his neck, making his vision swim slightly. This whole arrangement felt like a deliberate, calculated slight. He wanted to scream, to dismantle the plans, but the words caught in his throat, replaced by a tight, coiled tension in his chest. He slammed his hand flat onto the counter, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet apartment. "We agreed! Separate celebrations! Not a coordinated ambush!"

[story 2]

"This is ridiculous, Sarah," Liam muttered, pacing the length of the living room. His knuckles were white where he gripped the edge of the bookshelf. He could feel the blood pounding in his temples, a relentless, throbbing rhythm that matched the frantic beat of his heart. He watched her laugh, unconcerned, as she tied a ridiculously oversized bow onto a balloon. Every shared memory, every inside joke, suddenly tasted like ash in his mouth. He stopped pacing abruptly, planting his feet wide, and glared at the coordinated color scheme of the decorations. "You think this is funny? You think this is okay?" His voice was sharp, edged with a brittle quality that made the air around him feel suddenly thin. He took a deep, shuddering breath, trying to force the heat churning in his gut down, but it only intensified.

[story 3]

I watched them from the doorway, a cold knot tightening in my stomach. Maya was practically vibrating with excitement, humming off-key while setting up the cake. I could feel the pressure building behind my eyes, a relentless tightening that made focusing on anything else impossible. It wasn't just the logistics; it was the sheer audacity of it. They had planned this whole day as one, and now, with this sudden, unilateral decision, it felt like a profound betrayal. I walked over, my steps measured and deliberate, each one carrying a heavy weight. "Look at me," I commanded, my tone flat, devoid of warmth. Maya turned, her expression shifting from glee to confusion. "I just don't understand why you're making this so complicated." I didn't wait for an answer. I turned on my heel and walked out, the sound of my retreating footsteps heavy and uncompromising.
